era: 1959-1985. Radio/DJ. E=4 -- on-air presence energy. KJR 950 AM's golden age. Top 40 AM era. Pre-KCMU. Concert promotion. Beatles at Seattle Center. The mainstream radio king.

location.origin: Bremerton, Washington (born Paul Berg). Across Puget Sound from Seattle.
location.formative: KJR 950 AM studios, Seattle. The Top 40 AM radio ecosystem of the 1960s-70s. Seattle Center Coliseum (concert promotion). Spanish Castle ballroom (Highway 99). Puget Sound teen dance circuit.
location.career: KJR 950 AM (1959-late 1970s). Concerts West (concert promotion). Emerald Downs racetrack (later career). Seattle's most prominent DJ and concert promoter of the AM radio era.

members.name: Pat O'Day
members.note: Born Paul Berg in Bremerton, WA. Seattle's most famous Top 40 DJ. KJR 950 AM. Co-founded Concerts West. Promoted Beatles 1964 Seattle concert. Spanish Castle circuit. The AM radio king.

associated_musicians.name: Nasty Nes Rodriguez
associated_musicians.note: Both on-air DJs at E=4. Different eras (1960s vs. 1980s), different stations (KJR AM vs. KCMU FM), different genres (Top 40 vs. hip-hop). Same cultural role: DJ as performer and cultural force.

associated_musicians.name: Jonathan Poneman
associated_musicians.note: Poneman (KCMU, business, E=3) vs. O'Day (KJR AM, on-air, E=4). Behind-the-scenes vs. on-stage. Independent vs. commercial. Both built infrastructure.

associated_musicians.name: Bruce Pavitt
associated_musicians.note: Pavitt (KCMU, curatorial, E=3) vs. O'Day (KJR AM, promotional, E=4). Underground curation vs. mainstream promotion. Different filters, same goal: connecting music to audiences.

associated_musicians.name: The Beatles
associated_musicians.note: O'Day promoted the Beatles' August 21, 1964 concert at the Seattle Center Coliseum. 14,300 fans. O'Day introduced the band. One of the iconic moments in Seattle music history.

associated_musicians.name: Jimi Hendrix
associated_musicians.note: Hendrix played the PNW club circuit including the Spanish Castle in the early 1960s. O'Day promoted shows at the same venue. 'Spanish Castle Magic' (1967) immortalized the venue O'Day helped build.

theory_nodes.concept: Radio arc extends backward (126-129) -- four consecutive radio figures spanning from 1980s independent FM (KCMU) to 1960s commercial AM (KJR); the mainstream that the alternative defined itself against; the arc now covers both sides of the AM/FM cultural divide
examples: Nasty Nes (126, KCMU, hip-hop, E=4): independent FM performer.
Poneman (127, KCMU, business, E=3): independent FM architect.
Pavitt (128, KCMU, curatorial, E=3): independent FM curator.
O'Day (129, KJR AM, Top 40, E=4): commercial AM performer.
The arc documents both sides of the AM/FM divide.
theory_nodes.description: Radio arc extends backward. Four consecutive radio figures. KCMU FM (126-128) to KJR AM (129).

ancestors: KCMU/radio trio complete at 128. O'Day at 129 extends the arc into an earlier era.
The radio arc now spans from AM Top 40 dominance through FM independent revolution.
genealogy.relationship: Radio arc extends backward at degree 129. The mainstream context for the alternative.

theory_nodes.concept: Concert infrastructure foundation -- Concerts West proved Seattle was a viable major concert market; Beatles at Seattle Center (1964); Spanish Castle circuit; teen dance network; every promoter who followed inherited this infrastructure; the foundation under the grunge explosion
examples: Concerts West: national acts booked into Seattle Center venues throughout the 1960s-70s.
Beatles at Seattle Center Coliseum, August 21, 1964: 14,300 fans. O'Day introduced the band.
Spanish Castle ballroom: O'Day's circuit, Hendrix's inspiration.
Teen dances throughout Puget Sound: grassroots audience development.
theory_nodes.description: Concert infrastructure foundation. Concerts West. Beatles. Spanish Castle. Market proven.

ancestors: No prior concert promotion infrastructure documented in the engine.
O'Day establishes the foundation that all subsequent concert promoters and venue operators inherit.
genealogy.relationship: Concert infrastructure foundation at degree 129. The base layer for Seattle's live music ecosystem.

theory_nodes.concept: KJR/KCMU dialectic -- KJR AM (mainstream, commercial, Top 40, mass audience) vs. KCMU FM (independent, freeform, niche audience); the alternative could not exist without the mainstream; AM as thesis, KCMU as antithesis, KEXP as synthesis
examples: KJR AM: commercial, playlist-driven, advertiser-funded, mass audience, AM bandwidth.
KCMU FM: non-commercial, freeform, listener-funded, niche audience, FM fidelity.
The dialectic: mainstream creates the condition for alternative reaction.
KEXP as synthesis: independent ethos + professional infrastructure + global reach.
theory_nodes.description: KJR/KCMU dialectic. Mainstream vs. alternative. The thesis that created the antithesis.

ancestors: KCMU documented at degrees 126-128. KJR documented at degree 129.
The two stations represent opposite approaches to the same question: how should radio serve Seattle's music?
genealogy.relationship: Dialectic documented at degree 129. Mainstream (KJR) vs. alternative (KCMU).

theory_nodes.concept: DJ-as-celebrity era -- Top 40 AM radio DJs as local celebrities; O'Day recognized on the street, mobbed at events; the DJ was the show, not just the person who played records; E=4 on-air presence matching Nasty Nes (E=4) across eras
examples: O'Day's afternoon/evening shows drew massive audiences in the 1960s.
DJ voice as trained instrument: big, warm, rapid-fire, compressed AM sound.
KJR/KOL rivalry drove investment in personality DJs.
O'Day (1960s, AM, Top 40, E=4) and Nasty Nes (1980s, FM, hip-hop, E=4): same role, different eras.
theory_nodes.description: DJ-as-celebrity era. O'Day as local celebrity. The DJ was the show.

ancestors: The tradition of the charismatic radio DJ. Wolfman Jack, Alan Freed, Casey Kasem nationally.
In Seattle: O'Day was the local version of the national phenomenon.
genealogy.relationship: DJ-as-celebrity at degree 129. The tradition Nasty Nes inherited in a different genre.

cross_references.relationship: RADIO ARC! Both on-air DJs at E=4. O'Day (KJR AM, Top 40, 1960s) vs. Nasty Nes (KCMU FM, hip-hop, 1980s). Different eras, stations, genres. Same energy. Same role: DJ as cultural force.
RADIO ARC! Poneman (KCMU, business, E=3) vs. O'Day (KJR AM, on-air, E=4). Behind-the-scenes vs. on-stage. Independent vs. commercial. Both built infrastructure for Seattle music.

RADIO ARC! Pavitt (KCMU, curatorial, E=3) vs. O'Day (KJR AM, promotional, E=4). Underground curation vs. mainstream promotion. Different filters, different stations, same cultural role: connecting music to audiences.

CONCERT INFRASTRUCTURE! O'Day promoted Beatles at Seattle Center (1964). Spanish Castle circuit connects to Hendrix. Concert infrastructure O'Day built was the foundation for Seattle's live music ecosystem.
